method: identical 20 partitions & prompts; only reviewer model differs; same Haiku challenger verifies both; Opus adjudicates deltas

delta_findings.claim: get_version() does not handle PackageNotFoundError, causing module import to fail
delta_findings.opus_reason: get_version() is a function not module-level code; importing version.py never calls it

delta_findings.claim: Memo truncation slices at a hard character offset, ignoring line/paragraph boundaries, violating Markdown format.
delta_findings.opus_reason: memo.py hard-slice is intended length-cap with [truncated] marker, cosmetic nit

delta_findings.claim: False negative: The detector will miss database checks that use variable names instead of string literals for SQL.
delta_findings.opus_reason: _get_string_content only resolves ast.Constant strings, SQL via variable name not detected

delta_findings.claim: False positive on f-strings without interpolation (literal strings)
delta_findings.opus_reason: f-string with no interpolation parses to JoinedStr, flagged as dangerous formatting (FP in detector)

delta_findings.claim: CspellPlugin.run() relies on contextlib.suppress() to silently ignore JSON/KeyError/TypeError during parsing (line 77), then falls back to regex parsing. If the JSON reporter produces valid JSON but with unexpected schema (e.g. missing 'issues' key in future versions), plugin returns empty findings without logging, violating fail-open with diagnostics.
delta_findings.opus_reason: data.get never raises KeyError; silent empty-on-schema-drift is documented fail-open

delta_findings.claim: ReviewOptions.categories field is typed as list with no element type annotation, while scanners field is typed as list[str].
delta_findings.opus_reason: categories annotated list

delta_findings.claim: Timeout error message hardcoded to 60 even though no timeout parameter exists.
delta_findings.opus_reason: scancode passes timeout=60 to subprocess.run and error_msg uses 60; claim of no timeout false

delta_findings.claim: When `_looks_like_python` returns False (model returned non-Python output), `_try_model` immediately returns `None, attempts` without consuming the remaining retries, abandoning the model after one bad response rather than retrying.
delta_findings.opus_reason: solver returns (None,attempts) on non-Python output inside retry loop, abandons retries

delta_findings.claim: `_build_opa_input` emits only `id`, `severity`, and `message` for each finding, but the Rego policy rules for `critical_vuln`, `forbidden_license`, `malicious_package`, and `supply_chain_diff` all access `finding.category`, `finding.advisory_id`, `finding.package_name`, `finding.version`, and `finding.license_id` — fields that are absent from the OPA input built by this adapter, causing those deny/warn rules to never fire when `OpaRegoAdapter` is the active policy engine.
delta_findings.opus_reason: _build_opa_input emits only id/severity/message but rego reads category/advisory_id/etc

delta_findings.claim: merge_scribe overwrites all existing scribe fields with the new fields unconditionally via scribe.update(fields), so a second scribe silently clobbers values written by the first scribe for any key they share (e.g. both write "enclosing_symbol").
delta_findings.opus_reason: scribe.update(fields) unconditionally overwrites shared keys, later scribe clobbers earlier

delta_findings.claim: The canary-fail guard tests `canary_verdict.error` which is set to a non-empty string for ANY error, but `_review_one` also sets `error="LLM returned empty response"` for a transient empty response; this causes ALL remaining clusters to be permanently skipped for what may be a one-off timeout or rate-limit.
delta_findings.opus_reason: canary guard treats any truthy error (incl empty-response) as fatal, skips all clusters

delta_findings.claim: _call_and_validate loops `range(max_retries)` (0, 1) so it makes exactly max_retries=2 calls but the docstring says "Retries up to max_retries times" implying max_retries attempts after the first — callers get one fewer retry than they expect.
delta_findings.opus_reason: range(max_retries=2) only 2 attempts (1 retry), contradicts docstring "retries up to max_retries"

delta_findings.claim: `_orjson_dumps` is defined and `orjson` is imported but the function is never referenced anywhere in the codebase; the docstring's promise of "JSON round-trip support via orjson" is unimplemented.
delta_findings.opus_reason: _orjson_dumps defined but never referenced, orjson round-trip promise unimplemented dead code

delta_findings.claim: False positive — any function decorated with an attribute whose name contains "route", "get", "post", etc. (e.g., `@metrics.get_counter`, `@app.delete_user`) is detected as an API endpoint.
delta_findings.opus_reason: API_ENDPOINT_PATTERNS substring match, @metrics.get_counter matches via "get" (FP)

delta_findings.claim: False negative — `@lru_cache(maxsize=None)` is not detected; `maxsize=None` is explicitly unbounded but the guard only checks key presence, not value.
delta_findings.opus_reason: _has_unbounded_cache only checks key presence, @lru_cache(maxsize=None) treated as bounded

delta_findings.claim: False negative — a health endpoint identified by function name requires an exact route decorator (`"get"`, `"post"`, `"put"`, `"patch"`) but misses `@router.get(...)` (FastAPI-style, where the decorator name is `router.get`), causing the function-name path to never match real-world FastAPI health endpoints.
delta_findings.opus_reason: "route" in "router.get" is True and route-path /health matches; router.get not missed

delta_findings.claim: ClamAV's default timeout is 120s (double the project-mandated 60s scanner timeout from CLAUDE.md), and this default is hardcoded in the method signature rather than being read from config, so it silently violates the project invariant that `scanner=60s`.
delta_findings.opus_reason: clamav hardcodes default timeout=120, double the 60s mandate, not config-driven

delta_findings.claim: `_compute_first_published` returns the earliest upload timestamp across **all versions ever published**, not the upload date of the requested version. The OPA `package_age` rule uses this date to decide whether a package is "new" and should be denied. A freshly published version of a long-standing package (e.g. a typosquat releasing `requests==99.0.0` today) will report `package_age_days ≈ 4000` (since `requests` was first published in 2011) and pass the 30-day gate without restriction.
delta_findings.opus_reason: _compute_first_published returns earliest across all releases, new version of old pkg bypasses 30d gate

delta_findings.claim: `query_code` opens a SQLite connection (`sqlite3.connect`) but does not set a busy-timeout or WAL mode, and — more critically — if `conn.execute` raises an exception that is NOT `sqlite3.OperationalError` (e.g., `sqlite3.DatabaseError`, `sqlite3.ProgrammingError`), the exception propagates uncaught out of the function, breaking the fail-open contract documented everywhere else in the codebase.
delta_findings.opus_reason: nl_query catches only sqlite3.OperationalError, other sqlite exceptions propagate (fail-open break)

delta_findings.claim: Exception handler builds ReviewDecision with scan_results variable from outer loop scope, which may be stale if orchestrator.run has not completed or has failed.
delta_findings.opus_reason: scan_results bound at 169 before loop; inner except reached only after run() succeeds, never stale

delta_findings.claim: Both-versions-None case (old_ver is None and new_ver is None) defaults to "upgraded" action but should never occur — indicates the logic path is unreachable or the code is defensive against an impossible condition without documenting why.
delta_findings.opus_reason: both-None case inside elif old_ver!=new_ver, final else unreachable dead/defensive code

delta_findings.claim: DetectorFinding.to_finding() loses line_number and column information, breaking traceability to source code location.
delta_findings.opus_reason: to_finding() omits line_number/column; core Finding model has no such fields, location lost

delta_findings.claim: False positive on .format() with no arguments (safe parameterized queries)
delta_findings.opus_reason: flags any .format attribute call regardless of args, no-arg q.format() is FP

delta_findings.claim: CpdPlugin.run() catches all exceptions generically; runner call receives no timeout parameter; both error paths return PluginResult but generic Exception case doesn't include error_msg().
delta_findings.opus_reason: generic except DOES populate error=str(exc); claim's core defect is wrong

delta_findings.claim: Exception handler catches BLE001 (all exceptions) but logs at error level, contradicting fail-open philosophy.
delta_findings.opus_reason: handler returns needs_review (correct fail-open); only log level is error, no behavioral contradiction

delta_findings.claim: _run_mypy() and _run_pyright() are duplicated code paths with nearly identical structure (subprocess.run, timeout handling, JSON parsing logic). Violation of DRY; makes future bug fixes require changes in two places.
delta_findings.opus_reason: _run_mypy/_run_pyright duplicate filter+subprocess+TimeoutExpired boilerplate (minor DRY)

delta_findings.claim: Only `FindingCategory.license` findings are excluded from dedup; the remaining non-vulnerability categories (`copyright`, `code_smell`, `behavioral`, `security`, `supply_chain`, `age`, `transitive_count`, `malicious`, `malware`) all enter the dedup path keyed on `(advisory_id, category, package_name, version)`. For these categories `advisory_id` is virtually always `None`, so the effective key is `(None, "code_smell", "pkg", "1.0")` — meaning all distinct code-smell/behavioral/supply-chain findings for the same package@version collapse to a single entry, silently dropping real findings.
delta_findings.opus_reason: dedup key with advisory_id=None collapses distinct non-vuln detector findings, drops findings

delta_findings.claim: `OpaPlugin.description` hard-codes "6 Rego rules" but the policy contains 8 rules (5 deny + 3 warn per CLAUDE.md and policy.rego), making the plugin self-report an incorrect capability count that will diverge again whenever a new rule is added.
delta_findings.opus_reason: description hard-codes "6 Rego rules" while policy has 8

delta_findings.claim: LlmClient constructs a single shared httpx.Client with a fixed timeout baked in at __init__ time, but the timeout value stored in self._timeout (used for log messages) and the timeout on the actual HTTP client can diverge if config.llm_timeout is mutated after construction — and more concretely, the client is never closed unless the caller explicitly calls close(), creating a file-descriptor / connection-pool leak in any code path that constructs LlmClient in a short-lived scope.
delta_findings.opus_reason: LlmClient has only close(), no context manager/__del__, short-lived instance leaks pool

delta_findings.claim: The fallback-to-free-models logic is gated on `if result or self._is_anthropic`, which causes the fallback loop to be skipped entirely when the Anthropic backend is used and returns an empty string, silently dropping the response.
delta_findings.opus_reason: fallbacks are free OpenRouter models; skipping for Anthropic backend is intended

delta_findings.claim: `Remediator.remediate_finding` does not implement retry logic, unlike the shared `post_with_retry` used in `HolisticReviewer`, creating an asymmetry where audit fan-out retries on 429/5xx but remediation fan-out does not — a single transient rate-limit during remediation produces a permanent empty response and a "Skipped — canary failed" cascade.
delta_findings.opus_reason: remediate uses bare post with no retry while concern_review uses _post_with_retry

delta_findings.claim: _CRITICAL_HIGH is a plain set of raw strings rather than using the severity Enum that CLAUDE.md mandates for all state fields, breaking the project-wide invariant and making the set invisible to refactoring tools if the enum values ever change.
delta_findings.opus_reason: _CRITICAL_HIGH raw-string set vs severity values, contravenes enum-for-state convention

delta_findings.claim: False negative — `jwt.encode(payload={"sub": "x"}, key=k)` (payload passed as keyword arg) is silently skipped because the detector only inspects `call_node.args[0]` and returns `True` (safe) when `call_node.args` is empty.
delta_findings.opus_reason: empty args _has_audience_claim returns False producing finding; claimed mechanism opposite of code

delta_findings.claim: False positive in message — the function name is used instead of the decorator name, making the message misleading.
delta_findings.opus_reason: message uses decorated function name not decorator name, misleading

delta_findings.claim: False positive crash risk — `_has_half_open_config` iterates `call.keywords` and calls `keyword.arg.lower()` without guarding for `keyword.arg is None`; `**kwargs` unpacking in a call produces a keyword node with `arg=None`.
delta_findings.opus_reason: _has_half_open_config calls keyword.arg.lower() no None guard, **kwargs arg crashes AttributeError

delta_findings.claim: `tempfile.mktemp()` is a TOCTOU race — the file path is reserved but never created, so another process can create a file at the same path between the mktemp() call and gitleaks writing its output; on certain OS configurations gitleaks may refuse to write to a pre-existing path or the wrong file gets read.
delta_findings.opus_reason: gitleaks uses tempfile.mktemp(), TOCTOU-prone deprecated API

delta_findings.claim: `_SETUP_HOOK_FILES` membership is tested with `str.endswith(tuple)`, which is a string-suffix check, not a basename check; any file whose path ends with the characters `setup.py` or `setup.cfg` (e.g. `pkg/mysetup.py`, `lib/not_setup.py`) is incorrectly treated as a build-install hook file, producing false-positive critical `SC-INSTALL-HOOK` findings.
delta_findings.opus_reason: no _SETUP_HOOK_FILES/SC-INSTALL-HOOK/setup.py handling exists; cited mechanism does not exist

delta_findings.claim: `check_health` imports directly from `caliper.data.db` (the data layer), violating the cli→core→data import direction rule; the CLI layer must only import from `core/`, not from `data/`.
delta_findings.opus_reason: inspect_cmds imports from caliper.data.db in CLI layer, violates cli->core->data direction

delta_findings.claim: CdkNagPlugin.run() catches all exceptions generically without differentiating timeout/not-installed from other errors, violating fail-open contract that requires specific error handling.
delta_findings.opus_reason: cdk_nag catches Exception and returns PluginResult(error=str(exc)), exactly fail-open contract

delta_findings.claim: Identical silent close error suppression for pypi_client in evaluate_sbom method mirrors the same transparency loss as in evaluate().
delta_findings.opus_reason: pypi_client.close() in finally with suppress is standard idempotent cleanup, not a defect

delta_findings.claim: False negative when GITHUB_OUTPUT reference appears > 3 lines before heredoc
delta_findings.opus_reason: context window lines[max(0,i-4):i], GITHUB_OUTPUT >3 lines before heredoc not seen (FN)

delta_findings.claim: COUNT(*) queries assume rows always exist; will crash on empty database.
delta_findings.opus_reason: COUNT(*) always returns one row even on empty table, fetchone()["c"]=0 never crashes

delta_findings.claim: main() does not validate pr_number > 0 after conversion, allowing pr_number=0 to reach ForemanAgent.run() which uses it in API calls, potentially causing silent failures or incorrect routing.
delta_findings.opus_reason: main() validates pr_number==0 at line 243 and exits; cannot reach agent.run()

delta_findings.claim: False negative: The detector checks for @lru_cache() without maxsize, but will miss @lru_cache(maxsize=None) which is semantically unbounded in older Python versions.
delta_findings.opus_reason: @lru_cache(maxsize=None) has maxsize kwarg so not flagged yet unbounded (real FN)

delta_findings.claim: `ThreadPoolExecutor` is never shut down if any exception other than `TimeoutError` escapes the `as_completed` loop, leaking threads for the lifetime of the process.
delta_findings.opus_reason: as_completed loop only handles TimeoutError, no finally, non-TimeoutError leaks executor

delta_findings.claim: `_RETRYABLE_STATUS = {429, 500, 502, 503, 504}` includes 429, but the 429 case is explicitly handled earlier at line 318 and never reaches the `_RETRYABLE_STATUS` branch at line 330. The set definition silently implies 429 is handled by generic backoff when it is actually handled by `_extract_rate_limit` logic — creating a misleading contract for future editors.
delta_findings.opus_reason: 429 handled at 318 with continue, never reaches _RETRYABLE_STATUS at 330, dead/misleading

delta_findings.claim: The field `scan_time_bucket` is named to imply a time/duration bucket but actually stores a file-count bucket, creating a persistent naming contradiction that will mislead every reader of the telemetry schema and any downstream dashboard that labels the field.
delta_findings.opus_reason: field named scan_time_bucket but docstring says file-count bucket, Literal values are file ranges

delta_findings.claim: Absolute file URIs are never percent-encoded, so paths with spaces or non-ASCII characters produce malformed `artifactLocation.uri` values that violate the SARIF 2.1.0 spec.
delta_findings.opus_reason: _make_locations sets uri=str(file_path) no percent-encoding, paths with spaces produce bad uri

delta_findings.claim: The `errors` list in `run_audit` is built from ALL verdicts including the canary, but `ConcernVerdict.error` is a non-empty string for both "LLM returned empty response" and any other error string; this causes the `AuditReport.errors` list to duplicate errors reported by verdicts, and any consumer diffing `errors` vs `verdicts` will see mismatched semantics.
delta_findings.opus_reason: parallel path only sets error="LLM returned empty response", line 484 label accurate

delta_findings.claim: _DIM_RE matches any ALL_CAPS_UNDERSCORED token as a dimension name, so spurious lines in the LLM preamble (e.g. "NOTE: PASS — …" inside a code fence) silently consume a valid-looking line and could cause a duplicate-dimension error for a later legitimate line, or skip a required dimension without producing a missing-dimension error.
delta_findings.opus_reason: loop skips matches not in REQUIRED_DIMENSIONS, spurious token filtered, no dup/missing error

delta_findings.claim: `get_annotation_text` calls `get_call_name` to stringify an `ast.Attribute` annotation, but `get_call_name` requires an `ast.Call` node as its first argument and returns `None` for anything else — so `ast.Attribute` annotations (e.g., `pydantic.SecretStr`, `typing.Optional`) always produce `None`, silently failing type-annotation extraction.
delta_findings.opus_reason: get_annotation_text passes Attribute to get_call_name which returns None for non-Call, annotations stringify None

delta_findings.claim: `_HEREDOC_RE` requires the delimiter to start with an uppercase letter (`[A-Z]`) and contain only uppercase letters, digits, and underscores — it will miss lowercase heredoc delimiters like `<< 'eof'` or `<< delim` which are valid shell and equally vulnerable.
delta_findings.opus_reason: _HEREDOC_RE requires [A-Z], lowercase delimiters never matched despite being valid

delta_findings.claim: False negative — the window-based check allows an atomic marker anywhere within ±10 lines, including in a completely unrelated code block (e.g., a different function), to suppress a finding for a write call.
delta_findings.opus_reason: ±window scan accepts atomic marker anywhere in window incl unrelated block (FN)

delta_findings.claim: `target_files` declares `("*.py", "*.yaml", "*.yml", "*.json")` but the detector only performs Python AST analysis; YAML and JSON files always produce zero findings, making the declared scope misleading and config merges in YAML/JSON silently undetected.
delta_findings.opus_reason: detect() uses Python AST but declares yaml/yml/json targets, those yield zero findings (misleading scope)

delta_findings.claim: `_resolve_severity` CVSS score parsing treats the score as a plain float, but OSV `severity[].score` is a CVSS *vector string* (e.g., `"CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H"`), not a numeric score. The `float(str(score))` call will always raise `ValueError` which is suppressed by `contextlib.suppress`, silently falling back to `database_specific.severity` only.
delta_findings.opus_reason: OSV score is CVSS vector string, float() raises suppressed ValueError, numeric branch dead

delta_findings.claim: Unpinned-dependency findings for `requirements*.txt` files use `req.name` (the bare filename) as the `"file"` field, while `package.json` findings correctly use `rel` (the repo-relative path). In a monorepo with `backend/requirements.txt` and `frontend/requirements.txt`, all findings report `file="requirements.txt"` — indistinguishable.
delta_findings.opus_reason: npm findings use file=rel but requirements use file=req.name, monorepo reqs indistinguishable

delta_findings.claim: hmac.new() does not exist — the correct function is hmac.new() which is valid BUT the first argument must be bytes; secret.encode() returns bytes, which is correct, however the real issue is that the standard library function is `hmac.new()` — actually this is fine. Reconsidering: the actual bug is that `_verify_signature` calls `hmac.new()` which is NOT a stdlib function — Python's hmac module exposes `hmac.new()` as an alias for `hmac.HMAC()` in Python ≤3.9 but the canonical API is `hmac.new(key, msg, digestmod)`; this specific call is `hmac.new(secret.encode(), body, hashlib.sha256)` which is valid. Let me re-examine instead the `WebhookSettings.secret` field.
delta_findings.opus_reason: claim self-contradictory, concedes call valid; hmac.new is correct stdlib usage, no defect stated

delta_findings.claim: NameError when exception handler uses result.returncode but result is not in scope.
delta_findings.opus_reason: ValueError only from _parse_output after result assigned; subprocess.run won't raise ValueError pre-assignment, no NameError

delta_findings.claim: PolicyDecision verdict field expects string enum but constructed with raw string literals, fragile against enum changes.
delta_findings.opus_reason: PolicyDecision.verdict typed PolicyVerdict StrEnum but constructed with raw "needs_review" literals, bypasses enum

delta_findings.claim: False negative: The detector will not flag path construction in certain common patterns because the heuristic for identifying "path-related strings" is weak and can be bypassed.
delta_findings.opus_reason: literal-based heuristic is intrinsic detector design not a defect; vague opinion no concrete missed pattern

delta_findings.claim: Scanned file count double-counts when PMD breaks and fallback processes same files.
delta_findings.opus_reason: pmd FileNotFoundError fires on first language before scanned+=len, fallback never re-counts, double-count unreachable

delta_findings.claim: KubeLinterPlugin.run() catches all exceptions generically without differentiating error types; runner (kube_linter_runner.py) is a black box whose error structure is unknown, risking silent failures.
delta_findings.opus_reason: broad except Exception returning PluginResult with error is intended fail-open contract

delta_findings.claim: _is_config_key() uses substring matching ("port" in "exported") which is overly broad and will match unintended keys
delta_findings.opus_reason: _is_config_key uses any(k in key.lower()), "port" in "exported" True, substring over-broad

delta_findings.claim: `evaluate_sbom` never stamps `commit_sha` onto its `ReviewRequest` objects, so every request produced by the SBOM path stores `commit_sha=None` in the DB and in evidence, making audit trails and evidence seals untraceably detached from any commit.
delta_findings.opus_reason: evaluate_sbom never stamps req.commit_sha unlike evaluate() at 151, SBOM requests persist commit_sha=None

delta_findings.claim: `_clean_code` only removes the *first* opening fence encountered and only the single trailing fence; it does not handle multiple fenced blocks in one LLM response. Inner `` ``` `` delimiters from additional code fences survive into the returned code and will cause `ast.parse` to fail, which then falsely marks a valid-code response as non-Python.
delta_findings.opus_reason: _clean_code strips only first opening fence and single trailing fence, intermediate ``` survive break ast.parse

delta_findings.claim: `list_artifacts` only enumerates top-level files in the evidence key directory, but `create_seal` in `seal.py` hashes all files recursively via `rglob("*")`. A caller using `list_artifacts` to enumerate artifacts before passing them to `create_seal` will see a subset of what the seal covers, making the two APIs inconsistent and likely to produce an incorrect artifact count check.
delta_findings.opus_reason: list_artifacts uses iterdir() top-level only while seal hashes via rglob; nested SBOM keys diverge

delta_findings.claim: `_make_locations` silently clamps line 0 to line 1, masking zero-indexed or missing-line findings, AND produces an invalid SARIF `startLine` when `start_line` is a falsy non-zero value such as `0`.
delta_findings.opus_reason: or-chain swallows line 0 into 1 but SARIF startLine is 1-based so defensive; claim incoherent, debatable

delta_findings.claim: When a single oversized file exceeds `max_tokens_per_cluster`, it is appended to its own cluster with `name=key` (not `key:0`), but subsequent split chunks use `key:0`, `key:1`, etc., creating a name collision when that oversized file is the last file in an otherwise-splittable bucket.
delta_findings.opus_reason: single chunk named key, multiple key:0/key:1; no path produces both key and key:0, no collision

delta_findings.claim: _call_llm truncates the advisory with a hard byte-slice on a unicode string, which can produce a mid-codepoint cut and corrupt the last character of the advisory.
delta_findings.opus_reason: text[:N] slices str by codepoint not bytes, never cuts mid-codepoint, claimed mechanism nonexistent

delta_findings.claim: `ReviewReport.verdict` is typed as a bare `str` instead of an enum, violating the project invariant "enums for all state fields, never raw strings".
delta_findings.opus_reason: ReviewReport.verdict typed bare str (ports.py:173), violates enum-for-state invariant

delta_findings.claim: `file_path.read_text()` at line 66 is called outside the `try/except SyntaxError` block (lines 67–70), so an `OSError` (file not readable, permission denied) raises an unhandled exception instead of returning `[]`, violating the fail-open contract.
delta_findings.opus_reason: read_text() outside try (only catches SyntaxError), OSError propagates, violates detector fail-open

delta_findings.claim: False positive — `_needs_timeout` matches ANY function call whose name contains `"run"`, `"call"`, etc., not just subprocess calls; combined with `find_function_calls(tree, "subprocess.*")` being scoped to subprocess, this is internally consistent — but `_get_call_name` at line 108 returns non-subprocess names like `"proc.call"` or `"runner.run"` that satisfy `_needs_timeout`, yet the upstream pattern `subprocess.*` would already exclude them from the loop. The real bug is the opposite: `find_function_calls(tree, "subprocess.*")` uses the `get_call_name` utility which matches on the full dotted name, so `from subprocess import run; run(...)` (imported without the `subprocess.` prefix) emits `"run"` as the call name — `get_call_name` returns `"run"` (plain Name node) which does NOT match the `"subprocess.*"` glob, silently missing bare imported subprocess calls.
delta_findings.opus_reason: find_function_calls subprocess.* relies on dotted name, bare-imported run() missed (FP-claim actually FN, real defect)

delta_findings.claim: False negative — `_IF_GUARD_RE` matches `if.*advisory_id` on a preceding line but does not verify the `if` guard is a non-None check; `if advisory_id == expected_value:` (checking equality, not None-safety) would suppress the finding even though None is still a possible value that would collapse dedup keys.
delta_findings.opus_reason: _IF_GUARD_RE matches any if mentioning advisory_id incl non-None checks, suppresses though None possible

delta_findings.claim: `ghsa` field in findings uses `startswith("GHSA")` (no hyphen) but GHSA IDs always have the form `GHSA-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx` — the missing hyphen means `vuln_id.startswith("GHSA")` happens to work but is inconsistent with the `startswith("GHSA-")` pattern used for the URL in `_advisory_url` (line 61), creating a latent inconsistency if OSV ever introduces other GHSA-prefixed formats.
delta_findings.opus_reason: claim concedes startswith("GHSA") works; cosmetic inconsistency no incorrect behavior

delta_findings.claim: `_image_is_floating` misidentifies private-registry images of the form `registry.host:5000/image` (no version tag) as pinned, because the colon from the port number is treated as a tag separator and `"5000/image"` is not equal to `"latest"`.
delta_findings.opus_reason: _image_is_floating splits registry:port on colon, tag "5000/image"!=latest, wrongly returns pinned

delta_findings.claim: The OPA policy path is computed relative to bootstrap.py's location using four ".." traversals, but this assumes bootstrap.py lives exactly four directories deep under the repo root; if the package is installed (e.g. via pip/uv into site-packages), this resolves to a path inside site-packages/../../.. rather than the installed policies/ directory, silently producing a wrong path.
delta_findings.opus_reason: policy_path four-.parent traversal assumes source layout, resolves wrong in site-packages install

delta_findings.claim: WebhookSettings.secret is a required non-Optional field, but _load_app() calls WebhookSettings() without catching MissingConfigError for missing CALIPER_WEBHOOK_SECRET.
delta_findings.opus_reason: MissingConfigError does not exist; WebhookSettings() raises pydantic ValidationError; failing loudly on missing required secret at boot is intended

delta_findings.claim: Topological sort does not validate that a plugin's depends_on name actually exists; silently drops unknown deps.
delta_findings.opus_reason: dropping unknown depends_on names is documented intended behavior, correct for plugin-subset filtering

delta_findings.claim: False negative on secrets assigned without type annotations at module/class scope
delta_findings.opus_reason: detector only inspects AnnAssign so unannotated secrets missed, but annotation-only scope plausibly intended

delta_findings.claim: Timeout error message passes 0 instead of actual timeout value.
delta_findings.opus_reason: clamav line 69 passes literal timeout=0 into TIMEOUT error instead of in-scope timeout param (default 120)

delta_findings.claim: Severity bucket counts computed via three separate iterations over blocked list, O(3n) instead of O(n)
delta_findings.opus_reason: actionability 37-39 three separate sum() passes over blocked, O(3n) accurate

delta_findings.claim: BlastRadiusPlugin.run() assumes CodeGraph.stats() always returns a dict with 'symbols' key (line 70), but if CodeGraph is uninitialized or crashes, stats() may return empty dict or raise, causing unhandled exception.
delta_findings.opus_reason: stats() returns hardcoded literal dict always containing symbols; cannot be empty, _run_one fail-open anyway

delta_findings.claim: If `orchestrator.run()` raises (which it documents as "never raises" but can in theory if the executor itself cannot be constructed), the pipeline catches the exception in the per-package `except Exception` block only after the `scan_results` variable is bound — but `scan_results` is assigned before the loop so any exception during `orchestrator.run()` escapes the per-package handler entirely and propagates to the caller, violating fail-open.
delta_findings.opus_reason: structurally accurate but orchestrator provably never raises except ThreadPoolExecutor construction, theoretical

delta_findings.claim: The "### Why" section unconditionally re-renders `pol.triggered_rules[:5]`, which is a subset of the same rules already listed verbatim under "### Triggered Policy Rules" (lines 60-63). Identical content appears twice in every reject/needs-review memo, wasting space in the 3900-char budget and confusing readers.
delta_findings.opus_reason: reject/needs_review memo "### Why" triggered_rules[:5] duplicates rules under "### Triggered Policy Rules"

delta_findings.claim: `vendor` is in `_ALWAYS_SKIP_DIRS` but not in `DEFAULT_PATTERNS`, so `GitLsFilesSource` enumerates tracked files under `vendor/` while `WalkFileSource` prunes the directory entirely, making scan results differ depending on which source is active.
delta_findings.opus_reason: vendor in _ALWAYS_SKIP_DIRS but absent from DEFAULT_PATTERNS, GitLsFilesSource enumerates tracked vendor files, source divergence

delta_findings.claim: `calculate_quality_score` classifies any plugin that is NOT in `_SECURITY_PLUGINS` as a quality plugin, meaning unknown/new plugins (e.g. a future "supply-chain-v2" plugin whose name does not exactly match the hardcoded set) silently inflate the quality score rather than the security score, and the scores will disagree with the category-based verdict logic in `_build_sections` and `summarize_review`.
delta_findings.opus_reason: calculate_quality_score includes any plugin not in _SECURITY_PLUGINS, unknown new plugin inflates quality score

delta_findings.claim: The trust-verdict parser in `render_audit_markdown` matches "UNTRUSTED" before "TRUSTED", but since `"UNTRUSTED"` contains the substring `"TRUSTED"`, the `elif "TRUSTED" in tail` branch can never match a verdict that is genuinely "TRUSTED" when the word "UNTRUSTED" also appears anywhere later in the tail — causing TRUSTED verdicts to be miscounted as UNTRUSTED.
delta_findings.opus_reason: UNTRUSTED-before-TRUSTED is correct superstring-first ordering, claim has logic backwards

delta_findings.claim: REQUIRED_DIMENSIONS is a tuple, and `REQUIRED_DIMENSIONS.index(d.name)` is called inside a sort key for every dimension on every call — O(n²) scan on a fixed-size collection. More critically, if a dimension somehow slips through `found_dims` with a name that is not in REQUIRED_DIMENSIONS (e.g., due to a race between the `if name not in REQUIRED_DIMENSIONS: continue` guard and future code changes), `.index()` raises `ValueError` and crashes validation entirely, violating fail-open.
delta_findings.opus_reason: line-156 guard guarantees appended names in REQUIRED_DIMENSIONS, .index() cannot raise, claimed crash nonexistent

delta_findings.claim: `_derive_detector_id` calls `detector_class.detector_id.fget(None)` to extract the detector id without instantiation, but all concrete detectors declare `detector_id` as a `@property` that returns a string literal — `fget(None)` passes `None` as `self`, which works only because the property bodies are pure `return "CAL-XXX"` constants. If any future detector accesses `self` inside `detector_id` (e.g., for computed ids), `fget(None)` raises `AttributeError` silently swallowed by the `except (AttributeError, TypeError)` fallback, causing full instantiation. The silent fallthrough masks the error and makes registration non-deterministic across detector implementations.
delta_findings.opus_reason: fget(None) works for all detectors, except falls back to instantiation same id, no nondeterminism, hypothetical

delta_findings.claim: Path-segment check `"/agent/"` and `"/cli/"` uses simple substring matching on the full path string, causing false positives for files in unrelated paths that happen to contain `/agent/` or `/cli/` as a directory component (e.g., `vendor/third_party/agent/client.py`).
delta_findings.opus_reason: _PRESENTATION_SEGMENTS substring match seg in path_str on /agent//cli/, matches any path regardless of tier

delta_findings.claim: False negative — `.write_text(` in a string literal or comment triggers the marker check; lines like `# Use path.write_text(...)` or `doc = "call path.write_text("` are not code.
delta_findings.opus_reason: non_atomic_write line 76 raw-text substring marker in line no AST, .write_text( in comments/strings triggers (real defect)

delta_findings.claim: `_LATEST_TAG_RE` fires on `:latest` occurrences in Dockerfile comments, `RUN echo` strings, and `ENV` assignments that are not image references, producing false positives.
delta_findings.opus_reason: _LATEST_TAG_RE searched every line no FROM/comment/ENV filtering, fires on any :latest

delta_findings.claim: The `_make_relative` helper function is duplicated verbatim between swiftlint.py (lines 52-56) and swiftformat.py (lines 28-32), with no shared utility.
delta_findings.opus_reason: _make_relative byte-identical in swiftlint and swiftformat, no shared utility

delta_findings.claim: `ingest_lockfile` deletes ALL packages for a repo (`DELETE FROM repo_packages WHERE repo_name = %s`) before re-inserting, but the delete is scoped to the whole repo rather than the specific `lockfile_path` being ingested. A repo with multiple lockfiles (e.g., `requirements.txt` and `pyproject.toml`) will lose all packages recorded for every other lockfile whenever any single lockfile is re-ingested.
delta_findings.opus_reason: catalog DELETE FROM repo_packages WHERE repo_name repo-scoped not lockfile-scoped, no lockfile_path col, wipes other lockfiles

delta_findings.claim: OPA command argument order differs from policy.py, placing --format before data.policy instead of after, breaking CLI parsing.
delta_findings.opus_reason: OPA flag parser accepts positional query in any order; --format before data.policy does not break parsing

delta_findings.claim: _compute_diff hardcodes "upgraded" when Version parsing fails, ignoring actual version ordering — inconsistent with sbom_diff.py fallback which uses lexicographic comparison.
delta_findings.opus_reason: diff.py:212 hardcodes upgraded on InvalidVersion while sbom_diff:145 uses lexicographic fallback, loses downgrade

delta_findings.claim: Unsafe pattern: recommendation variable set to None outside conditional, then used in TaskFitAssessment construction only when errors is empty, violating explicit type contract
delta_findings.opus_reason: recommendation=None only in error branch, if errors returns early before construction, None never reaches constructor

delta_findings.claim: Redundant deduplication logic may mask multiple violations on same line
delta_findings.opus_reason: seen_lines dedup by line number is intentional; one finding per source line is correct not masking

delta_findings.claim: ComplexityPlugin.run() catches all exceptions generically without differentiating timeout from crashes; hard-coded timeout=60 in runner call is never overridden by config.
delta_findings.opus_reason: ComplexityPlugin.run() calls _run without timeout, default 60 never overridden by config; blanket except

delta_findings.claim: FakePolicyEngine.evaluate() returns bare verdict string, not DecisionVerdict enum
delta_findings.opus_reason: FakePolicyEngine returns verdict="approve" bare string where field typed PolicyVerdict StrEnum, violates enum convention

delta_findings.claim: stderr concatenated twice in redundant output parsing.
delta_findings.opus_reason: stderr concatenated in two distinct parse passes (findings/summary), both need stdout+stderr, not redundant

delta_findings.claim: Four symbols are re-exported from `pipeline.py` via a `# noqa: F401` star-import shim, but nothing in the codebase actually imports them from `pipeline` — making this a dead re-export that confuses the module's public API surface.
delta_findings.opus_reason: four pipeline_helpers symbols actually used inside pipeline.py, import is live, not dead re-export

delta_findings.claim: `needs_explanation` is False for `approve_with_constraints`, so a memo for a constrained approval renders the "### Constraints" section (line 103) with no preceding explanation of why constraints were imposed — leaving readers with a list of constraints and no context.
delta_findings.opus_reason: needs_explanation excludes approve_with_constraints, Constraints block renders without preceding Why explanation

delta_findings.claim: The lockfile search `break` statement fires whenever the first candidate lockfile name is present in the directory, even when `_is_within_repo` rejects it as a symlink escape, so no alternative lockfile is ever tried for that manifest.
delta_findings.opus_reason: lockfile loop break fires unconditionally once name in sibling_set even if _is_within_repo rejects, no alternate tried

delta_findings.claim: The comment truncation appends the overflow notice AFTER slicing to `_MAX_COMMENT_LENGTH - 100` bytes, but it appends a 51-character string; the final output is `_MAX_COMMENT_LENGTH - 100 + 51 = _MAX_COMMENT_LENGTH - 49` characters, not `_MAX_COMMENT_LENGTH`, so the comment is always 49 characters short of the limit when truncated.
delta_findings.opus_reason: truncating to MAX-100 then appending notice leaves comment under limit, headroom intended not defect

delta_findings.claim: `get_pr_diff_files` raises `RuntimeError` on non-zero `gh` exit code but `get_pr_diff_hunks` also raises `RuntimeError` on non-zero exit — both callers in the codebase must handle these exceptions, yet neither is documented as raising nor does `post_review` catch them; a failing `gh auth` or network error would propagate as an unhandled exception, violating the fail-open invariant.
delta_findings.opus_reason: sole caller cli/main.py:469-473 catches RuntimeError and exits, claim that no caller catches is false

delta_findings.claim: _is_actionable treats any non-empty fixed_version string as actionable, including sentinel values like "N/A", "none", "unknown", or "0" that some scanners emit when no fix exists, producing false positives in the actionable bucket.
delta_findings.opus_reason: only writer trivy emits real version or "", no scanner emits sentinels like N/A, claimed mechanism nonexistent

delta_findings.claim: `is_suppressed` reads the entire file from disk on every call, which is called per-finding in the scanner outer loop (scanner.py:143). For a file with N findings from M detectors, this is O(N) file reads of the same content — none of the suppression reads are cached.
delta_findings.opus_reason: is_suppressed does f.readlines() every call, scanner calls per-finding no caching, O(N) re-reads

delta_findings.claim: False negative — `@lru_cache` (bare, without parentheses) is not detected; only `@lru_cache()` with a call node is checked.
delta_findings.opus_reason: _has_unbounded_cache handles bare @cache and @lru_cache() but not bare @lru_cache, inconsistent detection gap

delta_findings.claim: False negative / false positive mix — `_has_ttl_check` uses a ±5 line proximity heuristic on line numbers derived from an AST walk of the entire file, not the enclosing scope; a TTL call in one function can suppress a finding in a nearby function.
delta_findings.opus_reason: _has_ttl_check walks whole-file tree with ±5 line proximity not scope-bounded, cross-function suppression

delta_findings.claim: `run_all` runs plugins in parallel via `ThreadPoolExecutor` even though `_topological_sort` establishes a strict execution order for `depends_on` constraints; the parallel execution ignores those ordering constraints entirely for plugins that share no explicit dependency edge.
delta_findings.opus_reason: run_all topo-sorts then ThreadPoolExecutor.map concurrent, depends_on ordering defeated for plugins sharing no edge

delta_findings.claim: SwiftLint severity values in the JSON output are title-case (e.g. "Warning", "Error") but `_SEV_MAP` uses only lowercase keys, so `.lower()` is applied before the lookup — but the map values are also raw strings ("error", "warning", "info") rather than normalized caliper severity levels ("high", "medium", "low"), producing non-standard severity strings in findings.
delta_findings.opus_reason: _SEV_MAP passes error/warning/info unchanged while codebase uses critical/high/medium/low/info, non-standard severity

delta_findings.claim: `decision_to_row` double-counts severity: it initialises `severity_counts` as a plain dict and then updates it via `severity_counts.get(f.severity.value, 0) + 1` — but `severity_counts[f.severity.value] = ...` would raise `KeyError` for any severity value not in the initial dict (e.g., `"unknown"` or a future enum variant), silently mis-counting rather than using the pre-seeded dict.
delta_findings.opus_reason: line 78 uses severity_counts.get(...,0)+1, unseeded key added gracefully, no KeyError no double-count, mechanism wrong

verdict: Sonnet found materially more real bugs: of the model-unique confirmed findings, Opus confirmed 54 sonnet-only bugs Haiku missed versus 16 haiku-only bugs Sonnet missed, and Sonnet did so at a far lower false-positive rate (19% vs 33%) AND ~15% fewer reviewer tokens. Sonnet's per-token price is higher, but on this codebase its 3.4x advantage in unique real bugs and lower noise make it clearly worth the higher price for a high-stakes review. Haiku remains a credible cheaper sweep that still surfaced 16 unique real bugs Sonnet overlooked, so the two arms are partly complementary.
